questions.text: The per-key state dict has no eviction. What is the expected key cardinality (e.g., per-IP, per-user-ID), and how will unbounded memory growth be managed over a long-running process lifetime?
After a long idle period the bucket silently refills up to full capacity before the next call — is this intentional burst behaviour, and should callers be warned about it?

The docstring marks this as single-process only: is this file purely a reference example, or is there a plan to promote it to production use where multiple replicas would require shared distributed state (e.g., Redis)?

answers.q1: Keys are per-user-ID with expected cardinality in the low thousands; unbounded growth is acceptable for this reference stub, but production would add a TTL/LRU eviction on the state dict.
answers.q2: Yes, the post-idle burst refill is intentional token-bucket behaviour; callers should be documented to expect up to a full burst after idle periods.
answers.q3: This file is a reference example only; promoting it to multi-replica production would require shared distributed state (e.g., Redis), which is explicitly out of scope here.
